coredata.dc:title: A laser free self-luminous nanosystem for photodynamic therapy of cervical cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Lin
Tan, Yiping
Zhang, Huaiyin
Guo, Pengyue
Yang, Dongzhi
coredata.dc:description: Photodynamic therapy is a tumor treatment strategy. However, most of the photodynamic therapies rely on laser irradiation triggering, which limits their application in deep tissues. This study designed a self-luminescent nano system, hybrid protein oxygen nanocarrier coated graphene quantum dots (GQDs@HPOC) and mesoporous silica nanoparticles coated Luminol (L@MSNs), which self-assembled into GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs without laser irradiation. The system utilized the weak acidic environment of tumors to trigger the release of Luminol and the chemiluminescence was catalyzed by HPOC. Next CRET occurred between Luminol and GQDs, producing 1O2, which could generate photodynamic damage to cervical cancer cells without the need for external laser irradiation. The system achieved the peak uptake in primary cervical cancer cells in 3 h, and had good biosafety before self-assembly. The system could significantly kill cells at a concentration of 16 μg/ml. The system will be further applied in in vivo experiments to investigate its therapeutic ability, providing a new strategy for the clinical treatment of cervical cancer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photodynamic therapy
GQDs
Luminol
Chemiluminiscence
Cervical cancer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Biosafety Evaluation of GQDs@HPOC and L@MSNs
In vitro therapy for primary cervical cancer cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: General Project of Natural Science of Jiangsu Province
Project of Science and Technology of Xuzhou Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by General Project of Natural Science of Jiangsu Province ( BK20201156 ), the Project of Science and Technology of Xuzhou Province (KC22116).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustrations of GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs preparations and its applications in cell treatment.
The SEM images of GQDs (A), MSNs (B) and GQDs@HPOC (C). The TEM images of L@ MSNs (D).

(A) The UV–Vis adsorption spectra of GQDs, HPOC and GQDs@HPOC. (B) The fluorescence spectra of GQDs and GQDs@HPOC. (C) The sulfydryl groups in HAS, Reduced-HAS, Hb, HPOC and GQDs@HPOC. (D) The Hb content of Hb, HPOC, GQDs@HPOC and GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs.
(A) The chemoluminescence spectra of Luminol and L@MSNs. (B) The zeta potential of Luminol, MSNs and L@MSNs (PBS, pH=6.5). (C) The curves of Luminol release from L@MSNs at different pH.

(A) The TEM images of GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs. (B) The diameter change of GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs in different solutions (H2O, DMEM and 10% FBS). (C)The UV–Vis adsorption spectum of GQDs and chemoluminescence spectrum of Luminol. (D)The 1O2 generating capacity of GQDs, GQDs@HPO, L@MSNs and GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs.
Cell biosafety evaluation. Cell viability and death staining images of L929 cells after incubation with different concentrations of GQDs@HPOC (A) and L@MSNs (B) for 24 h.
Cell viability and death staining images (A) and relation curves (B) of primary cervical cancer cells after incubation with different concentrations of HPOC/L@MSNs and GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs for 24 h.

Intracellular photodynamic performance. Fluorescence confocal imagings (A) and fluorescence intensity accumulation graphs (B) of HeLa cells co-cultured with different materials (PBS, GQDs@HPOC, L@MSNs, HPOC/L@MSNs, GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs and Rosup) for 3 h. The scale bar in the image is 20 μm.

head.ce:title: A laser free self-luminous nanosystem for photodynamic therapy of cervical cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangyin Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Wuxi, Jiangsu 214400, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangyin Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aJiangyin Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Wuxi, Jiangsu 214400, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of New Drug Research and Clinical Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, Jiangsu 221004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of New Drug Research and Clinical Pharmacy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bJiangsu Key Laboratory of New Drug Research and Clinical Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, Jiangsu 221004, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photodynamic therapy is a tumor treatment strategy. However, most of the photodynamic therapies rely on laser irradiation triggering, which limits their application in deep tissues. This study designed a self-luminescent nano system, hybrid protein oxygen nanocarrier coated graphene quantum dots (GQDs@HPOC) and mesoporous silica nanoparticles coated Luminol (L@MSNs), which self-assembled into GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs without laser irradiation. The system utilized the weak acidic environment of tumors to trigger the release of Luminol and the chemiluminescence was catalyzed by HPOC. Next CRET occurred between Luminol and GQDs, producing 1O2, which could generate photodynamic damage to cervical cancer cells without the need for external laser irradiation. The system achieved the peak uptake in primary cervical cancer cells in 3 h, and had good biosafety before self-assembly. The system could significantly kill cells at a concentration of 16 μg/ml. The system will be further applied in in vivo experiments to investigate its therapeutic ability, providing a new strategy for the clinical treatment of cervical cancer.

ce:section.ce:para: Cervical cancer is one of the most common malignant tumors among women in China, with the incidence rate ranking in the forefront of female malignancies. Although early detection, effective screening, and selective intervention can reduce the mortality rate of cervical cancer patients [1], the current mortality rate of cervical cancer still ranks fourth among female cancer types [2]. Currently, there are three main clinical traditional cancer treatments: surgery, chemotherapy, and radiation therapy. Chemotherapy is the most common cancer treatment method currently used, mainly by using chemical drugs to kill cancer cells [3]. However, due to the lack of tumor specificity, it usually causes significant damage to normal cells, making its efficacy not optimal. Although radiation therapy is a local treatment method, it also has a killing effect on normal cells. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a non-invasive treatment method that includes three components: laser, photosensitizer (PS), and oxygen [4,5]. The main process is that under laser illumination conditions, PS catalyzes oxygen to produce cytotoxic singlet oxygen (1O2), which can cause damage to tumor cells. Therefore, it has great potential in tumor treatment. PDT has the advantages of small trauma, low toxicity, good selectivity, and high repeatability, and is a promising tumor treatment method [6,7]. For patients who are intolerant to surgery, radiation therapy, and chemotherapy, PDT can be a good alternative. However, the traditional photosensitizers have poor water solubility, tissue and cell specificity, low 1O2 quantum yield, short ROS lifespan, and high phototoxicity, which severely hinder the clinical application of PDT [8]. Therefore, highly efficient photosensitizers with high 1O2 quantum yield and low toxicity have become a current research hotspot.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are carbon-based nanoscale particles with excellent chemical, physical, and biological properties, making them stand out in the wide range of applications of nanomedicine [9]. The unique electronic structure of GQDs imparts functional properties to these nanomaterials, including small molecular size, controllable fluorescence, bioactivity, photostability, high fluorescence emission, and photodynamic characteristics [10], such as adjustable photoluminescence for fluorescence bioimaging and biosensing [11]. Furthermore, they possess photocatalytic activity that can induce the production of long-lived singlet oxygen during the process of energy absorption and release under photo-irradiation, thereby exerting a cytotoxic effect on cells, while having biocompatibility under normal non-light conditions, making them suitable as photosensitizers for photodynamic therapy [12]. However, the tumor microenvironment differs from normal physiological ones, and limitations such as hypoxia and poor penetration of external light sources restrict the clinical application of photodynamic therapy. Thus, the improvement of the tumor microenvironment for oxygen deficiency and the provision of internal light sources within tumors have become urgent issues to be addressed [13].

Researchers have fabricated self-illuminating PDT systems through applying several self-illuminating systems, such as chemiluminescence or bioluminescence. In both, electronic excitations are generated and then transferred to the PS via Cherenkov Radiation Energy Transfer (CRET) or bioluminescence resonance energy transfer (BRET). The self-illuminating PDT eliminates the major shortcoming of PDT-based treatment, thus it can give new ideas for enhancing the overall yield of PDT [14–16]. In recent years, a variety of nanomaterials, including oxygen carriers (such as fluorocarbons and hemoglobin) and oxygen catalysts (such as MnO2 and CaO2), have been used to alleviate the hypoxic tumor microenvironment [17]. Regulating tumor hypoxia can also improve the therapeutic effect of PDT and reverse immune suppression in the tumor microenvironment [18]. Hemoglobin (Hb)-based oxygen carriers have been widely used in cancer treatment in recent decades due to their inherent reversible oxygen transport capabilities. However, free Hb is not an ideal oxygen carrier due to its short circulation time, poor stability, and potential side effects. Human serum albumin (HSA) is a highly soluble and abundant plasma protein that has been developed as a drug carrier. In addition, HSA has natural biocompatibility, good in vivo stability, and tumor-targeting ability. Some literature has proposed using a hybrid protein oxygen nanocarrier (HPOC) [19], which is formed by crosslinking HSA and Hb through intermolecular disulfide bonds, and loaded with Ce6(C@HPOC). Under laser irradiation, oxygen supply is improved by HPOC, which enhance the generation of cell toxicity 1O2 and trigger immunogenic cell death (ICD). Subsequently, the release of damage-associated molecules (such as CRT, HMGB1, DAMPs) and ATP after tumor cell death, promote dendritic cell (DC) maturation, which in turn activates T lymphocytes and natural killer (NK) cells. Therefore, HPOC-mediated PDT can not only destroy primary tumors but also inhibit distant tumors and lung metastasis through systemic anti-tumor immune responses. In addition, the newly validated efficient PDT is based on the spontaneous luminescence and oxygen self-supply system [9]. Hb, as a catalyst for the Luminol-H2O2 chemical luminescence system, also provides molecular oxygen, and the PDT process is triggered by external Luminol and H2O2. Luminol emits blue light (375–550 nm) in the presence of H2O2 and Hb, while NPs based on MH-PPV exhibit a broad absorption spectrum in the 400–550 nm range. The GQDs synthesized in this laboratory exhibit a broad absorption spectrum in the range of 350–550 nm. Therefore, the fluorescence energy can be transferred from Luminol to GQDS, sensitize oxygen molecules in Hb, and generate reactive oxygen species to kill cancer cells. This system provides a new method for PDT by combining internal activation of chemical luminescence and hypoxia compensation of oxygen self-supply.

In this project, GQDs@HPOC is synthesized using the nano-precipitation method while L@MSNs is synthesized using the Stöber method, and are electrostatically crosslinked to form GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs (Fig. 1 ). L@MSNs releases a large amount of Luminol in weakly acidic environments and under the catalysis of HPOC and H2O2, a chemical luminescence reaction occurs. Due to the strong positive charge of Luminol, it tightly binds to negatively charged GQDs@HPOC and forms CRET between GQDs, stimulating the fluorescence of GQDs. HPOC carries molecular oxygen and generates 1O2, killing tumor cells. The optical and structural properties of the composite nanocarriers, as well as their killing efficacy against tumor cells, were systematically studied in this project. The results confirmed their biosafety and the therapeutic effect of photodynamic therapy in vitro. Primary cervical cancer cells were cultured from collected clinical cervical cancer pathological tissues to verify their cytotoxicity against primary cervical cancer cells. The research content of this project not only provides experimental data for the application of GQDs as photosensitizers in photodynamic therapy, but also presents a novel treatment strategy by exploring Luminol as an internal light source for PDT.

N, N-dimethyldodecylamine, 4-bromobenzyl bromide and 3-thiophene boric acid were purchased from Alfa Aesar Chemical Co., LTD (China). Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), Pd(PPh3)4, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), triethanolamine (TEA) and 3-aminopropyl trethoxy silane (APTES) were bought from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD (China). Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium chloride (CTAC, 99%) and 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (APS, 97%) were obtained from Beijing Bailingwei Technology Co., LTD (Chia). 9, 10-anthracene disodium dipropionate (ADPA-Na2) was purchased from abcam Co., LTD (China). Fetal calf serum was bought from Zhejiang Tianhang Biotechnology Co. LTD (China). Column-layer chromatographic silica gel and Silica gel powder for TLC were purchased from Qingdao Marine Chemical Co. LTD (China). Bovine hemoglobin (Hb), human serum albumin (HSA) and reduced glutathione (GSH) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (China). Luminol (>98.0%) was gained from Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co., LTD (China). Hemoglobin detection kit was purchased from Shanghai Beibo Biotechnology Co., LTD (China). Other chemicals used in this work like NaOH, NaCl etc. were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (China) without further purification.

Scanning electron microscope images (SEM) were taken on scanning electron microscopy (Teneo, ThermoFisher Scientific,UAS), Transmission electron microscopy images (TEM) were recorded with a Tecnai G2 Spirit Twin transmission electron microscope operating (ThermoFisher Scientific, USA) at 200 kV. UV–Vis spectra were recorded from a MAPADA UV–6100 Spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China). N2 adsorption-desorption measurements were performed on a micromeritics instrument (ASAP2020). Mesopore size and specific surface area were detected using the BET Specific Surface Meter (Quadrasorb SI, Canta, USA). Fluorescence spectra were recorded by a FL4600 Fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). CCK-8 assay was performed using Varioskan LUX microplate reader (ThermoFisher Scientific, USA) at 450 nm. Fluorescence images were recorded using confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Lecia, Germany).

The synthesis of GQDs quantum dots was improved based on the existing synthetic methods of GQDs [20]. In this experiment, 0.5 g of 4-bromobenzyl bromide and 800 μL of N,N-dimethyldodecylamine were dissolved in a mixed solvent of CH2Cl2 and CH3OH (V:V = 3:2). The mixture was stirred at room temperature under N2 protection for 12 h. After the reaction, the solvent was removed by vacuum distillation, and the product was purified by recrystallization with ether and vacuum drying. The resulting white crystalline solid was compound 1.

400 mg of Pd (PPh3)4was used as a catalyst, and ethanoland double distilled water were used as solvents. Na2CO31.0 g, compound 1 0.8 g, and 3-thiopheneboronic acid 0.256 g were added under N2 protection, and the mixture was stirred and refluxed at 90 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, CH3CH2OH was removed by vacuum distillation, and the residue was extracted with 40 ml of CH2Cl2. The extract was combined and dried over anhydrous Na2SO4 to remove residual water. The residue was weighed, mixed with silica gel powder, and dry-loaded on a column. The column was eluted with CH2Cl2/CH3OH (V:V = 60:1). The target compound showed fluorescence under 254 nm UV light. The eluate containing the target compound was collected, and thin-layer chromatography was performed using CH2Cl2/CH3OH (V:V = 25:1) as the developing agent. The target compound was scraped off the silica gel layer, washed with methanol, and dried under vacuum to obtain white crystalline compound 2.

Compound 2 and four times its weight of anhydrous ferric chloride were dissolved in 20 ml of anhydrous chloroform, mixed well, and reacted at room temperature under N2 protection for 48 h. After the reaction, the precipitate was washed three times with methanol, dried, and obtained as a dark red solid, which was PT2. Then, PT2 (20 mg) was dissolved in 40 ml of double distilled water, and the pH was adjusted to 10 with NaOH. After ultrasonic dissolution, the supernatant was collected in a high-pressure reaction vessel and reacted at 160°C for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, and the filtrate was dialyzed to remove NaOH, yielding a neutral aqueous solution of GQDs.

According to the reference method [18], 20 mg of HSA was dissolved in 2 ml of double distilled water, and 6.8mg of GSH was added for reduction reaction at 37 °C for 1 h. The solution was then purified through centrifugation at 8000rpm for 15min, three times, using an ultrafiltration tube (3kDa) to obtain the reduced HSA solution. 4mg of Hb was dissolved in 1ml of double distilled water, and was slowly added to the above reduced HSA solution by using a microinjector. The pH was adjusted to 8.0, and the solution was stirred for 30 min after adding 1ml of CH3CH2OH. Finally, the solution was purified through centrifugation at 8000rpm for 15 min, three times, using a ultrafiltration tube (100 kDa) and then freeze-dried to HPOC. Then, 3.6 mg of Hb and 0.4 min of GQDs were used instead of 4mg of Hb, while keeping the other steps unchanged, to obtain GQDs@HPOC.

MSNs were synthesized using the Stöber method [21]. 1.0g of CTAC was dissolved in 10 ml of double distilled water and sonicated for 10 min until fully dissolved. 40mg of TEA was added and stirred at 90 °C for 30 min. 0.5 ml of TEOS was added dropwise using a microinjector, and the reaction was continued for 1 h. The resulting product was taken out and added to 0.636 g of Na2CO3 to continue the reaction for 30 min at 50 °C. After the reaction was stopped, the product was centrifuged (10000 rpm, 20 min) to remove the solvent, and washed three times with double distilled water and a methanol solution (1%) of sodium chloride. The product was then dried to obtain MSNs.

20 mg of MSNs were dissolved in 10 ml of CH3OH and sonicated until evenly dispersed. The solution was then heated to 90 °C, and 60 μl of APTES was added dropwise. The reaction was conducted under dark conditions for 12 h, and the reaction solution was centrifuged (10000 rpm, 20 min). After washing three times with CH3CH2OH, the product was dried to obtain amino-functionalized MSNs [22].

Synthesis of L@MSNs, referring to the existing method [23]: 4mg of Luminol was dissolved in NaOH solution (0.1%), and 10 mg of MSNs were dissolved in 5 ml of PBS and mixed evenly. The reaction was carried out under dark conditions for 24 h, and the reaction solution was centrifuged (10000 rpm, 20 min) and then dried to obtain L@MSNs.

HeLa cells were seeded at a density of 1.0 × 105 cells/ml in confocal culture dishes and cultured overnight until the cells completely adhered to the wall. Then, 400 μL of GQDs@HPOC and 400 μL of L@MSNs solution with the same concentration was added, and the cells were incubated for 0.5, 1, 2, 3, and 4 h, respectively. After that, the cells were washed with PBS for 3 times and fixed with 800 μL of 4% paraformaldehyde. After 10 min, the cells were washed with PBS and then incubated with 800 μL diluted lysosome green fluorescence probe (Lyso-Tracker Green) at 37 min°C for 30 min in the dark. The cells were washed again 3 times with PBS and 100 μL of PBS was added to maintain the cell morphology. Finally, fluorescence imaging was observed under a super-resolution laser scanning confocal microscope.

HeLa cells were seeded in confocal culture dishes at a density of 1.0 × 105 cells/ml and were cultured overnight until the cells were completely adhered to the wall. Then, solutions of GQDs@HPOC, L@MSNs, HPOC/L@MSNs, and GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs with the same concentration (800 μL) were added to the cells, respectively, and were incubated for 3 h. A negative control (PBS group) and a positive control (Rosup group) were set up simultaneously. After incubation, the cells were washed with PBS three times and were fixed with 800 μL of 4% polyformaldehyde. After 10 min, the cells were washed with PBS and were incubated with a fluorescent probe DCFH-DA (10 μM/L) diluted in serum-free DMEM medium at 37 °C for 20 min to label ROS. Then, the cells were fixed again with 1 ml of 4% polyformaldehyde, washed with PBS, stained with 3 μg/ml DAPI for 30 min at 37 °C, and finally washed with PBS three times and preserved in 100 μL PBS for observation under a super-resolution laser scanning confocal microscope.

All data were expressed as mean values. Statistical software IBM SPSS Statistics 20 was used for analysis. Variance analysis and t-test were used for statistical analysis, with p < 0.01 indicating statistical significance. All data were obtained from at least three parallel experiments.

Microscopic morphology characterization was carried out on the prepared nano-materials. As shown in Fig. 2 A, GQDs were similar to spherical particles, with uniform size distribution and an average particle size of about 10 nm [24]. They were uniformly distributed in the solution and their morphology was consistent with the literature. MSNs were prepared as shown in Fig. 2B, with a particle size of about 60 nm and a regular spherical shape, indicating the presence of a cavity structure that was uniformly distributed [25]. Surface mesopores were characterized, and the results shown in Figs. S1A and B revealed that the specific surface area of MSNs was 593.69 m2/g, and the mesopore diameter was about 4 nm, which can be used as a carrier to adsorb Luminol. TEM images of GQDs@HPOC (Fig. 2C) showed that it was similar to spherical particles, with a particle size of about 150 nm, and a uniform size distribution. Compared with GQDs, the particle size of GQDs@HPOC was significantly increased, suggesting the successful formation of a cross-linked composite. Fig. 2D showed that L@MSNs had a similar spherical shape with a particle size of about 60 nm, which was not significantly larger than MSNs. However, the surface mesoporous structure was concealed, possibly due to the Luminol wrapped and filled inside the pore [26]. The above results indicate that the required materials for the study have been successfully prepared, and their performance needs further verification.

The prepared HPOC, as shown in Fig. S2A, which exhibited characteristic absorption peaks of HSA at 279 nm and Hb at 405 nm, indicating the successful preparation of HPOC hybridized with Hb and HSA [20]. After GQDs were loaded onto HPOC, the characteristic absorption peaks of HPOC were still observed at 279 nm and 405 nm, while the GQDs peak at 490 nm was masked, possibly because the GQDs were mainly encapsulated inside (Fig. 3 A). The fluorescence spectra of GQDs before and after encapsulation were explored, as shown in Fig. 3B, with the emission wavelength of GQDs around 620 nm, and due to the quantum effect, the fluorescence spectra peak shifted to about 575 nm (EX.= 450 nm) after being loaded onto HPOC. As shown in Fig. S2B, the zeta potential of GQDs was negative, which was consistent with previous literature reports [27]. Additionally, due to the presence of free sulfhydryl groups (-SH) on the surface, HPOC exhibited strong electronegativity. After loading GQDs, GQDs@HPOC also demonstrated electronegativity [28]. To ensure the stability of the HPOC system, chemical cross-linking was used. Since HSA contained a large amount of disulfide bonds, the protein was hybridized using this bond connection method. The amount of thiol groups was determined by measuring the sulphur content, and it was found that when HSA was reduced by GSH, the amount of thiol groups increased significantly from 1.62 × 10−5 mol/L to 26.34 × 10−5 mol/L, indicating that the thiol groups in reduced HSA were consumed to form HPOC (Fig. 3C). The covalent cross-linking formed a stable structure with negative charge, which could be used as a carrier to transport drugs [29]. During the preparation of the material, Hb was easily affected, leading to a decrease in its content and activity, which determined the chemical luminescence performance of Luminol and further affected the ability of GQDs to produce active oxygen. Therefore, the Hb content was determined in the experimental process, as shown in Fig. 3D. The Hb content in GQDs@HPOC was about 30%, and it still reached 22.58% after being cross-linked with L@MSNs, providing a theoretical basis for realizing self-oxygen supply PDT. These results indicated that GQDs were successfully encapsulated in HPOC.

As shown in Fig. 4 A, the chemical luminescence peak of Luminol was about 423 nm, and after loading onto MSNs, it shifted to 418 nm while the wavelength and intensity remained almost constant, indicating that the chemical property of Luminol was not affected before and after loading. The surface charge of MSNs before and after preparing L@MSNs was measured and shown in Fig. 4B. Luminol is easily soluble in alkaline solutions, and when dissolved in PBS (pH = 6.5), it forms a suspension with positive surface charge. MSNs were nearly electrically neutral, but after drug loading, the surface charge of L@MSNs changed to + 75.79 mV due to the positive charge of Luminol. These data demonstrated the successful preparation of L@MSNs. The strongly positively charged L@MSNs could undergo electrostatic crosslinking with the electronegative GQDs@HPOC. The selective release performance of L@MSNs in the weakly acidic tumor microenvironment was verified, as shown in Fig. 4C. Luminol was rapidly released in different pH solutions within 6h, and the release rate slowed down after 6h, showing significant differences among three different conditions [30]. After 36h, the release rate of Luminol was only 9.4% in normal physiological pH 7.4 environment, while the release rates were 33.2% and 72.1% in pH 6.5 and 5.0 PBS buffer solutions, respectively. This indicated that L@MSNs could achieve pH-dependent release of Luminol. The chemical luminescence performance of Luminol could be activated by GQDs@HPOC under the weakly acidic tumor microenvironment, thus triggering the photodynamic therapy (PDT) performance of GQDs.

As shown in Fig. 5 A, after electrostatic cross-linking of GQDs@HPOC and L@MSNs, L@MSNs appeared black due to their different contrasts, while GQDs@HPOC appeared light in color. L@MSNs were connected outside of GQDs@HPOC and had a size of about 200 nm and a uniform distribution. The stability of the two materials in solution was further explored, as shown in Fig. 5B. The particle size was measured continuously for one week after dispersion in H2O, PBS, 10% FBS, and DMEM culture medium. Within 7 days, the particle size changes of GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs in the three media were all less than 1%, indicating that GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs had good dispersibility in the aforementioned solutions and were conducive to cell experiments. Fig. 5C shown that the Luminol spectrum peak range of Luminol was from 375 nm to 500 nm, while the UV absorption range of GQDs was from 350 nm to 550 nm. These two ranges overlap significantly, forming the basis for CRET and providing theoretical support for the use of GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs in PDT [24,31]. The verification of CRET between the two materials was shown in Fig. 5D. The UV–Visible absorption of ADPA-Na2 was quenched by 1O2, and the photodynamic performance of various materials was evaluated accordingly. Compared with a positive photosensitizer, Rose Bengal (RB), whose 1O2 quantum yield was 0.75 under light conditions, the 1O2 quantum yield of GQDs under light conditions was 0.71, while under light avoiding conditions, the 1O2 quantum yields of GQDs@HPOC, L@MSNs, and GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs were 0.08, 0.01, and 0.61, respectively, indicating that GQDs had photodynamic properties depending on light excitation [32]. GQDs@HPOC does not have the ability to produce 1O2 under light-avoiding conditions, nor does L@MSNs. Therefore, these results fully demonstrate that the chemical luminescence of Luminol can trigger CRET to excite GQDs to produce 1O2 after the combination of GQDs@HPOC and L@MSNs. These results also indicate that the materials have good safety before being combined and can only produce 1O2 to achieve photodynamic therapy after the formation of GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs. This hypothesis will be further validated through cell experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosafety Evaluation of GQDs@HPOC and L@MSNs
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, the biosafety of different concentrations of GQDs@HPOC and L@MSNs was qualitatively examined by live/dead staining analysis. The results, shown in Fig. 6 A and B, indicated that the red region marked dead cells and the green region marked live cells. For normal cells (L929), there were no obvious dead cells observed with an increase in the concentration of GQDs@HPOC or L@MSNs, and the cell status was good. The biosafety of different concentrations of GQDs@HPOC and L@MSNs was also quantitatively studied using the CCK-8 method. As shown in Fig. S3A, after incubating L929 cells with various concentrations of GQDs@HPOC or L@MSNs for 24 h, the cell survival rates were all greater than 80%. GQDs@HPOC and L@MSNs did not exhibit obvious toxicity to L929 cells. Furthermore, the safety of GQDs@HPOC and L@MSNs for tumor cells (HeLa) was also examined. The results of live/dead staining and CCK8 experiments were similar to their effects on normal cells (L929) (Figs. S3B–D), indicating good safety. These results demonstrate that GQDs@HPOC and L@MSNs have good safety before binding to each other, and the photodynamic catalytic activity of GQDs depends on the activation of chemiluminescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro therapy for primary cervical cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: This experiment qualitatively investigated the cytotoxic effects of different concentrations of HPOC/L@MSNs and GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs on primary cervical cancer cells through live/dead staining analysis. As shown in Fig. 7 A, there were no obvious dead cells and the cells remained in good condition when the concentration of HPOC/L@MSNs increased to 20 μg/ml. However, as the concentration of GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs increased, the red area gradually increased, and a large number of dead cells appeared. The CCK-8 assay was used to quantitatively study the effect of HPOC/L@MSNs and GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs on the survival rate of primary cervical cancer cells. As shown in Fig. 7B, when the drug concentration reached 20 μg/ml, the cell survival rate in the HPOC/L@MSNs group was still greater than 80%, while the cell survival rate in the GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs group significantly decreased when the concentration was greater than 16 μg/ml. There was a statistical difference (p < 0.01), indicating that GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs had a strong cytotoxic effect on primary cervical cancer cells. Different concentrations of HPOC/L@MSNs and GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs were co-cultured with Hela cells for 24 h to assess their ability to kill cells. As shown in Fig. S4, HeLa cells remained in good condition when the concentration of HPOC/L@MSNs increased to 20 μg/ml, while the GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs group killed a large number of cells when the concentration was 16 μg/ml, consistent with the cytotoxic effect on primary cervical cancer cells. These results indicate that GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs have excellent cell-killing effect and concentration dependence. Based on the results, it is speculated that the photodynamic production of 1O2 by GQDs may be the main reason for cell-killing and will be further verified.

In this experiment, the properties of the fluorescent probe DCFH-DA were used to detect the production of 1O2 inside cells. DCFH, which has no fluorescence, can be oxidized by oxygen to produce fluorescent DCF, so the fluorescence of DCF can determine the level of 1O2 within cells. Based on the uptake of GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs by cells (Fig. S5A), Lyso-Tracker Green was used for specific fluorescence staining of lysosomes, which showed green and marked the cytoplasm. GQDs and luminescent materials were separately excited to produce red and blue fluorescence. In HeLa cells, no significant red or blue fluorescence was observed at 0.5h, but with the extension of time, red and blue fluorescence in the cells gradually increased. The fluorescence intensities were analyzed using ImageJ software, and by 3h, the accumulation of GQDs and luminescent materials was time-dependent (Fig. S5B). At 4h, the content of GQDs and luminescent materials inside cells decreased due to efflux, thereby selecting 3h as the co-incubation time (Fig. S5C). As shown in Fig. 8 A, after DCFH-DA probe entered cells, it was oxidized by GQDs to produce 1O2, which showed green fluorescence. Compared with the positive control Rosup group, it still had strong fluorescence, and there was no significant green fluorescence in other groups. This indicated that chemiluminescence of luminescent materials was the only way for GQDs to produce 1O2, and GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs had good safety before being crosslinked with luminescent materials, which was consistent with the safety test results [33]. The fluorescence intensities were integrated using ImageJ software, as shown in Fig. 8B. Groups such as PBS, GQDs@HPOC, L@MSNs, and HPOC/L@MSNs showed little or no 1O2 production, while GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs and Rosup had high 1O2 production rates. At the cellular level, resonance energy transfer occurred in GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs, and it had a high ability to produce 1O2, which was the main factor in killing cancer cells [34]. The results show that Luminol excites GQDs to produce 1O2, making the material safe before binding and having potential for further use in live cervical cancer treatments.

In this study, GQDs@HPOC and L@MSNs were successfully synthesized and self-assembled in solution into GQDs@HPOC/L@MSNs nanostructures. The system could release a large amount of Luminol in the weakly acidic environment of the tumor, and chemically luminesce with the catalysis of Hb, undergoed CRET with GQDs, producing active oxygen. The nanosystem did not require laser irradiation and had good biosafety before binding. After electrostatic assembly, the accumulation concentration of the nanosystem reached its peak after co-incubating with cells for 3 h, and at a concentration of 16 μg/ml, significant cytotoxicity was observed on HeLa cells and primary cervical cancer cells. The cytotoxic effect was mainly due to the 1O2 generated by the photodynamic action of the nanosystem. The nanosystem shows significant advantages in vitro and can be further studied for in vivo treatment and provide new methods for cervical cancer therapy.
We are sure this manuscript has not been published elsewhere in whole or in part, and all authors agree with the submission.

Lin Wu: Writing – original draft, Project administration. Yiping Tan: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Huaiyin Zhang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Pengyue Guo: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology. Dongzhi Yang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by General Project of Natural Science of Jiangsu Province (BK20201156), the Project of Science and Technology of Xuzhou Province (KC22116).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.pdpdt.2023.103756.
